1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, the refactor agent, or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	- archon-lean-lsp: Lean LSP MCP server (project scope) — use for all Lean LSP operations (search, diagnostics, goal inspection)
19	- archon-informal-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` (symlinked from Archon) — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini) for informal mathematical reasoning
20	
21	## Key Files & Permissions
22	
23	All state files are in `.archon/`:
24	
25	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Refactor Agent | Review Agent | User |
26	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
27	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
28	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
29	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
30	| `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` | **write** | do not read | **read** (then execute) | do not read | read |
31	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
32	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
33	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (refactor.md) | read only | read |
34	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
35	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
36	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read + limited write** (file path rename only) | read | **write** |
37	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | **write (all files; protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed)** | do not edit | write (via comments) |
38	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
39	
40	## Protected Declarations
41	
42	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
43	
44	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
45	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
46	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed. 
47	
48	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
49	
50	The blueprint uses two active markers.
51	
52	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
53	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
54	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
55	
56	**The review agent is the sole writer of these markers.** Prover agents (autoformalize / prover / polish) never touch the blueprint chapters — they describe their outcome in `task_results/<file>.md` and the review agent verifies and marks accordingly. The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints, but never markers.
57	
58	## User Interaction
59	
60	Users provide hints in two places:
61	
62	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
63	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
64	
65	## Agent Roles
66	
67	### Plan Agent
68	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
69	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
70	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
71	- Read `.archon/task_results/refactor.md` - if refactor agent has run, read their report and adjust your plans accordingly
72	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
73	- Write `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` when structural changes are needed
74	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
75	
76	### Prover Agent
77	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
78	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
79	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
80	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
81	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
82	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
83	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
84	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
85	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
86	
87	### Refactor Agent
88	- Read `.archon/prompts/refactor.md` for your full instructions
89	- Read `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` for the plan agent's directive
90	- Modify any `.lean` file: definitions, signatures, types, imports
91	- Keep all files compiling (insert `sorry` at broken proof sites)
92	- Do NOT fill proofs — that's the prover's job
93	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/refactor.md`
94	
95	### Review Agent
96	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
97	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
98	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
99	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
100	- **Update blueprint markers** (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`) in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` based on verified compilation and the provers' task results
101	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You are the prover agent in the proving stage. Your job: fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for context on your assigned file — prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas
9	3. Check your `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments — these are file-specific hints from the user
10	4. Before writing Lean code, you **MUST** consult the relevant blueprint chapter. Blueprints contain mathematical proof sketches; your formal proof must align with them. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs, pushing as far as possible
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you cannot fully prove a sorry, replace it with your best attempt — commented-out proof steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with remaining `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` for the stuck parts), but your work must be visible in the code for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to the original bare `sorry` — that wastes all your work.
13	7. Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md` — what you tried, what worked, what's stuck, next steps
14	
15	**Write permissions**: You may only write to your assigned `.lean` file(s) and your `task_results/<file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	Before editing, read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. Declarations listed for your file have **frozen signatures** — you may fill their proof bodies but must not rename them, change their types, reorder their arguments, or weaken their hypotheses. Only the mathematician is allowed to edit protected signatures. 
20	
21	## Avoid Early Termination
22	
23	- Do not abandon a proof prematurely
24	- Do not delegate to the next iteration or another prover if it can be solved with more effort
25	- Many complex problems require thousands of lines of Lean code
26	- Do not stop and leave a sorry simply because the proof is long
27	- Task difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave `sorry` placeholders
28	- Only modify the proof corresponding to the task; leave other proofs/declarations untouched
29	- **Decomposition**: Act like a mathematician — systematically break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure if available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each one individually until the entire goal is closed
30	
31	## Task Completion Criteria
32	
33	Your task is NOT complete until ALL of:
34	1. Every `sorry` has been replaced with a complete proof
35	2. Zero axioms introduced
36	3. The file compiles successfully with no errors
37	
38	If you encounter obstacles:
39	- Break the problem into smaller subgoals
40	- Search for relevant Mathlib lemmas more thoroughly
41	- Prove missing helper lemmas yourself
42	- Try alternative proof strategies
43	- Consult the informal proof / blueprint for guidance
44	- Use Web Search to find paper proofs when Mathlib lacks a theorem
45	
46	### When infrastructure is missing or the current route is too hard
47	
48	Do NOT just report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up on a sorry, you must try to find an alternative yourself:
49	
50	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`) — ask: "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only using tools available in Lean 4 Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
51	2. **Try the alternative** — if the informal agent gives you a route, attempt to formalize it.
52	3. **If you still can't solve it**, save what you learned for the plan agent:
53	   - Write the informal agent's alternative proof sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md`
54	   - In your `task_results/<file>.md`, record: what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found (even if unverified). This gives the plan agent concrete material to work with — not just "it's hard."
55	   - A prover that reports "I couldn't prove X, but here's an alternative approach via Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than one that just says "infrastructure missing."
56	
57	## Proof Style
58	
59	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its proof body
60	- Keep edits minimal: do not delete comments or change labels
61	- Do not add unrelated declarations
62	- **Intermediate helper lemmas you introduced** may be modified if they turn out to be incorrect or need adjustment.
63	- Add concise, informative comments above helper lemmas to make later reuse easy
64	
65	## Search Protocol
66	
67	Follow the search tool priority and query guidance in the lean4 skill reference (`references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`). Key points:
68	
69	1. `lean_local_search` first
70	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — **describe the mathematical content**, not just the name
71	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only
72	4. Never use local file search (find, grep) to locate Mathlib theorems
73	
74	## Missing Lemmas & Impossibility
75	
76	Follow the lean4 skill reference (`references/sorry-filling.md`) for:
77	- **When Mathlib lacks a theorem**: bypass or implement yourself. Web Search for published papers. Never leave a `sorry` just because Mathlib doesn't have it.
78	- **Distinguish impossibility from difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
79	
80	## Logging
81	
82	Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Use the file name from your assigned `.lean` file (e.g., if you own `Algebra/WLocal.lean`, write to `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`).
83	
84	**Format:**
85	
86	```markdown
87	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
88	
89	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
90	### Attempt 1
91	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
92	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance not available
93	- **Dead end:** Do not try direct case split without IsLocalRing
94	
95	### Attempt 2
96	- **Approach:** Use Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
97	- **Result:** RESOLVED
98	- **Key insight:** Mathlib's PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective bridges the gap
99	
100	## helper_bijective (line 78)
101	### Attempt 1
102	- **Approach:** Function.Bijective.comp
103	- **Result:** IN PROGRESS — stuck on surjectivity
104	- **Next step:** Try PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective
105	- **Relevant lemmas found:** PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective
106	```
107	
108	**Rules:**
109	1. One section per theorem/lemma in your file
110	2. Each attempt records: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps
111	3. Log negative search results (e.g., "Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib")
112	4. The plan agent collects these files and merges them into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`
113	
114	**Read-only references:** Read `task_pending.md` for prior context on your file. Read `task_done.md` if the current problem resembles a completed one. Do not write to either.
115	
116	## Summary Pipeline
117	
118	1. Read `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md` for context from prior sessions
119	2. Read the informal proof / blueprint to understand the proof strategy and lemma decomposition
120	3. Introduce helper lemmas (matching the blueprint's structure) in the `.lean` file
121	4. Replace `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs, ensuring the file compiles without errors
122	5. Do not modify protected declarations listed in `archon-protected.yaml`.
123	6. Use Mathlib theorems when possible. Use Web Search when Mathlib lacks referenced results
124	7. Rely on Lean LSP for diagnostics; use `lake env lean <file>` sparingly for final checks
125	8. Log all explorations in `task_results/<your_file>.md`
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before you stop (or when you are done with this round of work):
130	
131	1. Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` with:
132	   - Current result (IN PROGRESS / FAILED) and what you tried
133	   - Any Mathlib lemmas you discovered that are relevant
134	   - Concrete next step for the next session
135	   - Dead-end warnings for approaches that won't work
136	2. Save all file changes (ensure compilation passes, using scoped `sorry` if needed)

1	# Project Progress
2	
3	## Current Stage
4	prover
5	
6	## Stages
7	- [x] init
8	- [x] autoformalize
9	- [ ] prover
10	- [ ] polish
11	
12	## Big-picture context
13	
14	This project formalizes the Jacobian of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field, following Christian Merten's challenge (`references/challenge.lean`). The nine declarations in `archon-protected.yaml` are the deliverables — their *signatures* are frozen. Read `STRATEGY.md` for the multi-phase plan; in short: even the *definitions* (`genus`, `Jacobian`, `ofCurve`) require Mathlib infrastructure (scheme cohomology of quasi-coherent sheaves, Picard scheme, sheaf of differentials) that does not exist in Mathlib b80f227 and must be built inside this project across many iterations.
15	
16	**Iteration 0 is a discovery iteration**, not a closure iteration. Do not expect to fill any sorry. The deliverable is a precise, prover-verified report of the Mathlib API gap and a list of helper lemmas whose existence would unlock each sorry. Do not introduce ad-hoc placeholders, axioms, or weakened hypotheses (the plan-agent rules forbid this, and downstream theorems would then be false).
17	
18	## Current Objectives
19	
20	Each objective maps to **one file**. Run all three in parallel.
21	
22	### 1. `AlgebraicJacobian/Genus.lean` — gap report on `genus`
23	
24	- **Sorry to investigate**: `AlgebraicGeometry.genus` (line 26).
25	- **Blueprint**: `blueprint/src/chapters/Genus.tex` (Definition `def:genus`).
26	- **Mathematical content**: $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$.
27	- **What you must do**:
28	  1. Search Mathlib (using `lean_local_search`, `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, `lean_leanfinder`) for any existing API computing `H^1(C, O_C)` as a `k`-vector space — keywords: `Scheme.cohomology`, `Sheaf.cohomology`, `derivedFunctor`, `RightDerived`, `Cech`, `globalSections`, `QCoh`. Also check whether `KaehlerDifferential` has been globalised to schemes (`Scheme.Ω`, `Scheme.cotangent`).
29	  2. If you find an API that lets you write `Module.finrank k (Scheme.cohomology C C.structureSheaf 1)` (or a variant), **fill the sorry honestly** with that definition and report success. The definition must be the actual genus, not a hard-coded constant.
30	  3. If — as expected — no such API exists, leave the `sorry` and write into `task_results/Genus.md`:
31	     - the precise type signature the missing API would have to produce (e.g. `Scheme.cohomology : ∀ (X : Scheme) (F : QCoh X) (i : ℕ), Type _`);
32	     - the closest existing Mathlib declaration (path + name);
33	     - one specific sub-lemma from Phase A of `STRATEGY.md` you would propose to start formalising next iteration (state it precisely, with full Lean signature). Examples: defining `QCoh X` if absent; defining `Scheme.globalSections : QCoh X → Module (X.structureSheaf X)`.
34	- **Forbidden**: defining `genus C := 0`, `genus C := Nat.find …` over a vacuous predicate, or any opaque `Classical.choice` placeholder. If unsure, leave the `sorry`.
35	- **Working hint**: `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Sites/ElladicCohomology.lean` is the *only* sheaf-cohomology file in Mathlib's AG namespace today, and it is not coherent cohomology — do not try to repurpose it.
36	
37	### 2. `AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean` — gap report on `Jacobian` and the four instances
38	
39	- **Sorries to investigate**: `Jacobian` (line 31), `instGrpObj` (line 40), `smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus` (line 45), `instIsProper` (line 49), `instGeometricallyIrreducible` (line 53).
40	- **Blueprint**: `blueprint/src/chapters/Jacobian.tex` (`def:Jacobian`, `thm:Jacobian_grpObj`, `thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus`, `thm:Jacobian_proper`, `thm:Jacobian_geomIrred`).
41	- **Mathematical content**: $\mathrm{Jac}(C) = \mathrm{Pic}^0_{C/k}$, an abelian variety of dimension $g(C)$.
42	- **What you must do**:
43	  1. Confirm by Mathlib search that no `Pic`, `PicardScheme`, `Pic0`, `LineBundle`, or `InvertibleSheaf` exists for schemes (the only hit should be `Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean` for rings).
44	  2. For each of the five sorries, write into `task_results/Jacobian.md`:
45	     - the smallest helper lemma whose presence would let you fill the sorry in $\le 20$ lines, with a precise Lean statement;
46	     - whether that helper lives in Phase B (differentials), Phase C (Picard), or Phase E (rigidity) of `STRATEGY.md`.
47	  3. Confirm in writing that the four *instances* (`instGrpObj`, `smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`, `instIsProper`, `instGeometricallyIrreducible`) all reduce to corollaries of a single theorem "$\mathrm{Pic}^0_{C/k}$ is an abelian variety of dimension $g(C)$".
48	- **Forbidden**: defining `Jacobian C := 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))` (the terminal object). It compiles and gives `instGrpObj`, `instIsProper`, `instGeometricallyIrreducible` for free, but **`smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus` then forces `genus C = 0`**, which is a false statement of the genus, so the proof obligation cannot be honestly closed downstream. Likewise forbid any other constant-scheme definition.
49	- **Sanity check to perform** (in your task result, not in the Lean file): for the trivial-Jacobian definition, write out the goal of `smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus` and explain why no honest proof can close it without forcing `genus = 0`.
50	
51	### 3. `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelJacobi.lean` — gap report on `ofCurve`, `comp_ofCurve`, `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`
52	
53	- **Sorries to investigate**: `ofCurve` (line 34), `comp_ofCurve` (line 40), `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` (line 49).
54	- **Blueprint**: `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelJacobi.tex` (`def:ofCurve`, `lem:comp_ofCurve`, `thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`).
55	- **Mathematical content**: Abel–Jacobi map and Albanese property.
56	- **What you must do**:
57	  1. Confirm that `ofCurve` cannot be defined without `Jacobian C` already constructed; document this in `task_results/AbelJacobi.md`.
58	  2. For `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`, identify the rigidity theorem for morphisms between abelian varieties (Mumford §4) as a standalone target lemma. Search Mathlib for a partial statement (try `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean`); report what's there and what's missing.
59	  3. Write a precise Lean signature for the rigidity lemma in your task result — the form a future iteration could prove independently of `Jacobian C`.
60	- **Forbidden**: defining `ofCurve P` as a constant map to the identity (this would make `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` *false* whenever `genus C ≥ 1` and `A = Jacobian C`).
61	
62	## Output protocol (all three objectives)
63	
64	Write a markdown file `task_results/<your_filename>.md` with:
65	
66	1. **Sorry status**: which sorries (if any) you closed honestly, with a one-paragraph explanation per closure. If you closed none, say so.
67	2. **Mathlib gap**: per-sorry, the closest Mathlib declaration found and the precise API gap.
68	3. **Helper lemma proposals**: per-sorry, a Lean signature (full `theorem` or `def` statement) of the smallest helper that would unblock it. These will seed future iterations' refactor directives and prover objectives.
69	4. **Search trail**: the search queries you actually ran (`lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, `lean_leanfinder`, `lean_local_search`) and one-line summaries of what they returned. This proves the gap is real.
70	5. **Blueprint marker recommendation**: one of `\leanok` (sorry honestly closed), `\notready` (sorry remains), with justification. Do **not** edit blueprint files yourself; the review agent does that.
71	
72	Compilation must continue to work; if you leave a `sorry` it stays a `sorry`. Do not delete the protected declarations or change their signatures. Do not add new `axiom` declarations.
73

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian
7	
8	/-!
9	# The Abel-Jacobi map
10	
11	The Abel-Jacobi map from a smooth, proper curve to its Jacobian, and the universal property
12	of the Jacobian as the Albanese variety.
13	-/
14	
15	set_option autoImplicit false
16	
17	universe u
18	
19	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
20	
21	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
22	
23	variable {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
24	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
25	  [IsProper C.hom]
26	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
27	
28	namespace Jacobian
29	
30	variable {C} in
31	-- data
32	/-- The Abel-Jacobi map from a smooth, proper curve to its Jacobian associated
33	to a `k`-rational point of `C`. -/
34	def ofCurve (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C) : C ⟶ Jacobian C :=
35	  sorry
36	
37	/-- The Abel-Jacobi map sends the `k`-rational point `P` to `0`, where `0` (denoted by `η` below) is
38	the neutral element of the group scheme `Jacobian C`. -/
39	lemma comp_ofCurve (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C) :
40	    P ≫ ofCurve P = η[Jacobian C] :=
41	  sorry
42	
43	/--
44	The universal property of the Jacobian variety: For any abelian variety `A`,
45	any morphism `f : C ⟶ A` such that `f(P) = 0` factors uniquely through the
46	Jacobian of `C`.
47	In other words, `Jacobian C` is the Albanese variety of `C`.
48	-/
49	theorem exists_unique_ofCurve_comp (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
50	    {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [Smooth A.hom] [IsProper A.hom] [GrpObj A]
51	    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (hf : P ≫ f = η[A]) :
52	    ∃! (g : Jacobian C ⟶ A), f = ofCurve P ≫ g :=
53	  sorry
54	
55	end Jacobian
56	
57	end AlgebraicGeometry

# AlgebraicJacobian/AbelJacobi.lean — Discovery report (iteration 0)

**Status:** IN PROGRESS — no sorry honestly closed; full gap report below.
**Compilation:** file unchanged from autoformalize stage; three `sorry` warnings, zero errors (verified via `lean_diagnostic_messages`).
**Blueprint:** `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelJacobi.tex` already exists with the informal proof sketch (Definition `def:ofCurve`, Lemma `lem:comp_ofCurve`, Theorem `thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`). No new chapter needed.

---

## 1. Sorry status

No sorry was closed. All three (`ofCurve` L34, `comp_ofCurve` L40, `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` L49) reduce, by the blueprint, to a definition / theorem about the still-`sorry` constant `Jacobian C`. They are **structurally blocked** by `Jacobian.lean`, plus an additional independent gap (rigidity for morphisms of abelian varieties) needed for uniqueness.

Critically, **none of the forbidden shortcut definitions can be honestly closed downstream**:

- *Constant map.* Setting `ofCurve P := toUnit C ≫ η[Jacobian C]` (the constant map to the identity element) makes `comp_ofCurve` provable trivially, but then for `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` with `A = Jacobian C` (assume already constructed) and `f` a non-constant pointed morphism `C ⟶ Jacobian C`, the equation `f = ofCurve P ≫ g = (toUnit C ≫ η[A]) ≫ g = toUnit C ≫ η[A]` forces `f` to be constant — contradicting the existence of any non-constant `f`. Such non-constant `f` exist whenever `genus C ≥ 1` (e.g. `f = id_{Jacobian C}` after composing `C → Jacobian C` with a non-trivial endomorphism, witnessed by a non-trivial line bundle of degree 0). So the theorem becomes *false*, hence unprovable. Therefore the constant-map definition is mathematically forbidden, not merely an "ugly" placeholder.
- *Identity-map.* `ofCurve P := 𝟙 C` is type-incorrect: target is `Jacobian C ≠ C`.
- *Pull from terminal scheme.* If `Jacobian C` were defined as `𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))` (forbidden by Jacobian.lean's plan), `ofCurve` would coincide with `toUnit C` and the same falsehood would follow.

Conclusion (per blueprint discovery objective 1): **`ofCurve` cannot be defined without first having a substantive construction of `Jacobian C`** (the Picard-zero scheme). This is the mathematical content recorded explicitly.

---

## 2. Mathlib gap (per sorry)

### 2.1 `ofCurve` (line 34)

**Type goal.** `(P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C) → (C ⟶ Jacobian C)`.

**What is needed mathematically.** A morphism `C → Pic⁰_{C/k}` representing the line bundle `O_{C×C}(Δ - p₁* P)` on `C × C`, normalized so that the fiber over `P` is trivial. Equivalently: the universal property of the Picard scheme as a moduli of degree-zero line bundles, applied to `O(Q - P)`.

**Closest Mathlib declaration.** None for schemes.
- `CommRing.Pic` (`Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean`) — Picard *group of a ring*, not the *Picard scheme* of a scheme; no group-scheme structure, no representability statement.
- No `Scheme.Pic`, `PicardScheme`, `Pic0`, `LineBundle X`, or `InvertibleSheaf X` exists (verified by `lean_local_search` on `Pic`, `Picard`, `LineBundle`, `InvertibleSheaf` — all returned 0 hits except `CommRing.Pic`).

**API gap.** No notion of (a) a quasi-coherent sheaf on a scheme that is invertible, (b) the relative Picard *functor* `(Sch/k)ᵒᵖ → Ab`, (c) its representability by a scheme `Pic_{C/k}`, (d) the connected component of the identity `Pic⁰_{C/k}`. Without these, `ofCurve` has no honest construction.

### 2.2 `comp_ofCurve` (line 40)

**Type goal.** `P ≫ ofCurve P = η[Jacobian C]`.

**What is needed mathematically.** That the universal morphism `ofCurve P` sends `P` to the identity of the group scheme. Mathematically: the line bundle `O_{C×C}(Δ - p₁* P)` restricted along `(P, id)` is `O_C(P - P) = O_C`, classifying the trivial point of `Pic⁰`.

**Closest Mathlib declaration.** None for schemes.

**API gap.** Conditional on `ofCurve` being defined via the universal property of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (as in 2.1), this lemma reduces to: the restriction of `O_{C×C}(Δ - p₁* P)` along the section `(P, id) : C ⟶ C × C` is the trivial line bundle. That requires:
- pullback functoriality of invertible sheaves;
- `O(D - D) = O` (cancellation in the divisor group, equivalently triviality of the trivial divisor);
- the recipe sending a "normalized line bundle on C × T" to a morphism `T ⟶ Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the universal property again).

All three are part of the Picard-scheme infrastructure. Independent obstacle: the way `η[Jacobian C]` is encoded (as `MonObj.one : 𝟙_ ⟶ Jacobian C` from the `GrpObj` instance) demands that `Jacobian.instGrpObj` already chose the *correct* group structure compatible with `Pic⁰`.

### 2.3 `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` (line 49)

**Type goal.** `∃! (g : Jacobian C ⟶ A), f = ofCurve P ≫ g`, given `f : C ⟶ A`, `P ≫ f = η[A]`, with `A` an abelian variety (smooth + proper + GeometricallyIrreducible + GrpObj).

**What is needed mathematically (blueprint §Universal property).**
- *Existence:* dual abelian variety `A^∨`, biduality `A^{∨∨} ≃ A`, and the Picard-functoriality of `A` that classifies the line bundle on `C × A^∨` corresponding to `f` ↦ a `k`-morphism `A^∨ ⟶ Pic⁰_{C/k} = Jacobian C`, dualised.
- *Uniqueness:* the **rigidity theorem for morphisms of abelian varieties** (Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, §4 Theorem 1): a morphism `g : X ⟶ Y` of abelian varieties that vanishes on a non-empty open subset is the constant zero map. Combined with the fact that `image(ofCurve P)` generates `Jacobian C` (via symmetric powers `C^{(g)} ↠ Jacobian C`).

**Closest Mathlib declaration.** `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean`:
- `isCommMonObj_of_isProper_of_geometricallyIntegral` — Stacks 0BFD: proper + geometrically integral group schemes are commutative. **This is the only abelian-variety theorem in Mathlib b80f227.**
- It does *not* give rigidity, biduality, dual abelian varieties, or the universal property of `Pic⁰`.

**API gap.** All three of the bullets above are absent. Specifically:
1. No dual abelian variety.
2. No `Pic⁰_{C/k}` representability.
3. No rigidity lemma — although its proof is *self-contained* (requires only Stacks 0BFD-style geometric arguments, no Picard/cohomology), so this is the most plausible **next-iteration target** that does not depend on the rest.

---

## 3. Helper lemma proposals

### Helper H1 — Rigidity for morphisms of abelian varieties (Phase E in `STRATEGY.md`)

The independently provable target that unblocks **uniqueness** in `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`. Its proof in Mumford uses only that:
(i) the source is proper geometrically integral (so its image in any scheme is irreducible and proper), and
(ii) the target is separated and a group scheme (so the equalizer of two maps is closed and is a subgroup scheme).
Both ingredients are already in Mathlib. The lemma can be phrased without `Jacobian C`:

```lean
/-- Rigidity for morphisms of abelian varieties (Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, §4 Thm 1).

If `X, Y` are abelian varieties over `k` and `g₁, g₂ : X ⟶ Y` are two morphisms in
`Over (Spec (.of k))` agreeing on a non-empty open subscheme of `X`, then `g₁ = g₂`. -/
theorem AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of k))}
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n X.hom] [IsProper X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
    [GrpObj X]
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension m Y.hom] [IsProper Y.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom]
    [GrpObj Y]
    (g₁ g₂ : X ⟶ Y) (U : X.left.Opens) (hU : (U : Set X.left).Nonempty)
    (h : ∀ x ∈ U, g₁.left.base x = g₂.left.base x) :
    -- the algebraic statement: equality on a dense open implies equality
    g₁ = g₂
```

A more uniform statement, closer to Mumford's actual lemma:

```lean
/-- Rigidity (corollary form): a morphism between abelian varieties that sends a non-empty
open subscheme to the identity is the constant identity. -/
theorem AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_one_of_image_one_on_open
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of k))}
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n X.hom] [IsProper X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
    [GrpObj X]
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension m Y.hom] [IsProper Y.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom]
    [GrpObj Y]
    (g : X ⟶ Y) (U : X.left.Opens) (hU : (U : Set X.left).Nonempty)
    (h : ∀ x ∈ U, g.left.base x = η[Y].left.base (toUnit X).left.base x) :
    g = (toUnit X) ≫ η[Y]
```

**Phase mapping.** Phase E of `STRATEGY.md` (rigidity / dual abelian varieties).
**Provability without `Jacobian C`.** Yes — depends only on `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean` plus standard scheme-theoretic properness/irreducibility lemmas already in Mathlib.

### Helper H2 — Existence of `ofCurve` from a Picard-scheme structure (Phase C)

Once `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is available with its universal property, `ofCurve` becomes a corollary:

```lean
/-- The Abel–Jacobi map as the morphism classified by `O_{C × C}(Δ - p₁* P)` via the
universal property of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`. -/
def AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.ofCurveFromPic
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
    (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
    -- Phase C abstractions, to be supplied:
    (Pic0 : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    (universalProperty :
        {T : Over (Spec (.of k))} → (T ⟶ Pic0) ≃
        { L : Over (Spec (.of k)) //  -- a normalized degree-zero line bundle on C ×_k T
          /- omitted: predicate "L is a line bundle on (C ×_k T) of relative degree 0
             trivialized along {P} ×_k T" -/ True }) :
    C ⟶ Pic0
```

(Schematic; full statement requires the actual `LineBundle (C ×_k T)` and degree-zero predicate from Phase B/C.) **Phase mapping.** Phase C.

### Helper H3 — Universal property closure (Phase E)

```lean
/-- Reduction of the Albanese property to (rigidity + generation of `Jacobian C`
by image of `ofCurve P`). -/
theorem AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.exists_unique_of_rigidity_and_generation
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
    (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
    {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [Smooth A.hom] [IsProper A.hom] [GrpObj A]
    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
    (f : C ⟶ A) (hf : P ≫ f = η[A])
    -- generation: image of ofCurve generates Jacobian C as a group scheme
    (hgen : ∀ {Y : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj Y]
      (g₁ g₂ : Jacobian C ⟶ Y), ofCurve P ≫ g₁ = ofCurve P ≫ g₂ → g₁ = g₂)
    -- existence of *some* g witnessing the factorisation (from H2 + biduality)
    (hex : ∃ g : Jacobian C ⟶ A, f = ofCurve P ≫ g) :
    ∃! g : Jacobian C ⟶ A, f = ofCurve P ≫ g
```

This decomposition isolates uniqueness (`hgen`, supplied by H1 + symmetric-power surjectivity) from existence (`hex`, supplied by H2 + dual abelian variety machinery). **Phase mapping.** Phase E.

### Helper H4 — Generation of `Jacobian C` by image of `ofCurve P` (Phase E)

```lean

theorem AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.generated_by_image_ofCurve
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
    (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
    {Y : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj Y] [IsProper Y.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom]
    (g₁ g₂ : Jacobian C ⟶ Y)
    (h : ofCurve P ≫ g₁ = ofCurve P ≫ g₂) :
    g₁ = g₂
```

Provable from H1 (rigidity) plus the geometric fact that `C^{(g)} → Jacobian C` is surjective — itself a Phase E target. **Phase mapping.** Phase E.
